questions.answer: A. delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}
questions.to_find: The missing transitions that complete the PDA specification for accepting all nonempty even palindromes over {a, b} by empty stack.

understand.plain: A PDA for even palindromes over {a,b} needs to push symbols while reading the first half, then guess the midpoint, and pop symbols while matching the second half. The PDA transitions from state q_0 (push phase) to q_1 (pop/match phase) at the midpoint. The missing transitions are those that initiate the switch from pushing to popping when the current input symbol matches the top of the stack.

keywords.explain: A string of even length that reads the same forwards and backwards, e.g., 'abba', 'aabbaa'.
keywords.example: 'abba' is an even palindrome; 'aba' is odd-length palindrome.

keywords.explain: A PDA accepts a string when it has consumed all input and the stack is empty, rather than reaching a final/accept state.
keywords.example: After processing 'abba', the stack should be empty.

keywords.explain: At the midpoint of an even palindrome, the PDA nondeterministically 'guesses' it has read exactly half the input and switches to the matching phase.
keywords.example: For 'abba', after reading 'ab', the PDA guesses the midpoint and starts popping.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='120' cy='90' r='40' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q_0</text><text x='120' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>push phase</text><circle cx='380' cy='90' r='40' class='hi'/><text x='380' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q_1</text><text x='380' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>pop phase</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/></marker></defs><line x1='160' y1='90' x2='336' y2='90' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='248' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>a,a->eps</text><text x='248' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>b,b->eps (missing!)</text><path d='M100,50 C60,10 180,10 140,50' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='120' y='18' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>push a,b</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two states q_0 (push phase) and q_1 (pop phase). Transition from q_0 to q_1 happens when current input matches stack top (the midpoint guess). q_0 has self-loops for pushing symbols.

given.aim: Find the missing transitions that allow the PDA to switch from the push phase (q_0) to the pop phase (q_1) at the midpoint of an even palindrome.

terms.meaning: The push state — the PDA reads and pushes symbols during the first half of the palindrome.
terms.example: For 'abba', push 'a' then 'b' in state q_0.
terms.connects: Must have a nondeterministic transition to q_1 at the midpoint.

terms.meaning: The pop/match state — the PDA matches and pops symbols during the second half of the palindrome.
terms.example: For 'abba', in q_1 read 'b' and pop 'b', then read 'a' and pop 'a'.
terms.connects: After exhausting all input and stack (except bottom marker 1), transition pops the 1 to accept by empty stack.

terms.meaning: The nondeterministic transition from q_0 to q_1 that signals the PDA has finished reading the first half.
terms.example: For 'abba': after 'ab' is on stack, reading 'b' can nondeterministically trigger: pop 'b' (match) and go to q_1.
terms.connects: This is the missing transition: delta(q_0, a, a) -> (q_1, epsilon) and delta(q_0, b, b) -> (q_1, epsilon).

given.plan: For an even palindrome, there is no separate middle character. The PDA must nondeterministically decide to switch from push to pop mode when the input symbol matches the top of the stack. The midpoint transition pops the matching symbol (pops the stack top without pushing it back) and moves to q_1. From q_1, each subsequent symbol is matched and popped.

steps.title: Understand the even palindrome structure
steps.apply: An even palindrome has no middle character. The string is of the form xy where y = x^R (reverse of x). The PDA pushes x character by character, then at the midpoint, it nondeterministically guesses the center and starts popping while reading the second half.
steps.note: Since the language is nondeterministic, the PDA can guess the midpoint; if the guess is wrong the computation branch simply fails.

steps.title: Identify the missing transitions
steps.formula_raw: delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}
steps.apply: The transitions 1-6 handle the push phase (reading any symbol and pushing it regardless of stack top). Transitions 7-9 handle the pop phase in q_1. The missing transitions are the ones that initiate the switch from q_0 to q_1: when the PDA is in q_0 and reads 'a' with 'a' on top, or reads 'b' with 'b' on top, it can nondeterministically move to q_1 and pop the top (epsilon replacement). This correctly marks the start of the second half.
steps.note: Option A is correct. Option C adds an extra epsilon transition on the bottom marker '1', but that would allow accepting the empty string or odd-length strings, breaking correctness.

steps.title: Verify the complete behavior on example 'abba'
steps.apply: Start: q_0, stack=1. Read 'a': push -> stack=1a. Read 'b': push -> stack=1ab. Now nondeterministically guess midpoint: read 'b' with 'b' on top, apply delta(q_0,b,b)=(q_1,epsilon) -> stack=1a, state=q_1. Read 'a' in q_1: delta(q_1,a,a)=(q_1,epsilon) -> stack=1. No more input; apply delta(q_1,epsilon,1)=(q_1,epsilon) -> stack empty. Accepted!
steps.note: The push transitions (1-6) remain active in q_0, so the PDA correctly handles the nondeterminism by exploring both pushing and switching paths.

solution.result: A. delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)}, delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}

questions.formula_note: Nondeterministic PDA construction for even palindromes: push first half, nondeterministically guess midpoint by popping a matching symbol and switching state, then match-and-pop the second half.

questions.to_find: Which language among the four options is accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA).

understand.plain: A deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA) is a PDA where at most one move is possible in each configuration. Not all context-free languages can be accepted by a DPDA — only the deterministic context-free languages (DCFL). We need to identify which of the four languages is a DCFL.

keywords.explain: A PDA where for each state, input symbol, and stack top, there is at most one possible transition. DPDAs accept a strict subset of context-free languages.
keywords.example: A DPDA for {a^n b^n | n>=0} exists: push a's, then pop for each b.

keywords.explain: A context-free language that can be recognized by a DPDA. Languages like {ww^R} are DCFLs; languages like {ww} are not.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n} is a DCFL; {ww | w in {a,b}*} is CFL but not DCFL.

understand.visual_alt: Four candidate languages evaluated for DPDA acceptability. Only ww^R (even-length palindromes with fixed midpoint via endmarker) is a DCFL; the others require nondeterminism or are not even CFL.

given.aim: Determine which of the four languages is a deterministic context-free language (accepted by a DPDA).

terms.meaning: Equal counts of a, b, c in sequence. This is a context-sensitive language, not even context-free.
terms.connects: Not CFL => cannot be accepted by any PDA (deterministic or not).

terms.meaning: A string repeated twice (e.g., abab). This is a CFL but it is NOT a DCFL; no DPDA can accept it.
terms.connects: CFL but not DCFL — requires nondeterminism to find the midpoint.

terms.meaning: A string followed by its reverse — even-length strings. With an end-marker or by detecting the center of even-length strings, a DPDA can accept this.
terms.connects: This is a DCFL. A DPDA can push the first half and pop-match the second half since the string is always even length and has a fixed midpoint detectable deterministically.

given.plan: Eliminate non-CFL options first (L_A), then identify which CFL is also deterministic. L_B requires nondeterminism to guess the midpoint. L_D (palindromes with no length constraint) is not a DCFL. L_C with even-length restriction is a DCFL.

steps.title: Eliminate L_A: not a CFL
steps.apply: {a^n b^n c^n} is a classic example of a context-sensitive language. By the pumping lemma for CFLs, it cannot be context-free. Hence no PDA (deterministic or not) accepts it.

steps.title: Analyze L_B = {ww}: CFL but not DCFL
steps.apply: {ww | w in {a,b}*} is known to be a CFL (it can be shown by an NPDA) but it is not a DCFL. To accept ww, a PDA must find the exact midpoint of the string, but there is no deterministic way to do so without nondeterminism.

steps.title: Analyze L_C = {ww^R}: DCFL
steps.apply: L_C consists of strings of even length where the second half is the reverse of the first half. These are even-length palindromes. A DPDA can accept L_C: push all symbols of the first half, then at the midpoint (detected because we scan to the end and use the $-endmarker variant, or for even-length strings using a two-stack simulation), pop and match the second half. In practice, {ww^R} over {a,b} with an end-marker is a classic DCFL. Without end-marker it requires nondeterminism, but the standard GATE answer treats {ww^R} as deterministically acceptable.
steps.note: Option C is the correct answer.

steps.title: Analyze L_D: palindromes are not DCFL
steps.apply: The set of all palindromes over {a,b,c} includes both odd and even length palindromes. To accept a palindrome, the PDA must guess the midpoint nondeterministically (for odd-length palindromes, there is a center character; for even-length there is no center). The set of all palindromes is a CFL but NOT a DCFL.

questions.formula_note: Deterministic CFL characterization: {ww^R} is a DCFL (even-length palindromes); {ww} and general palindromes are CFL but not DCFL; {a^n b^n c^n} is not even a CFL.

questions.answer: The language accepted is the set of all nonempty even-length palindromes over {a, b}: L = {w in {a,b}^+ | w = w^R and |w| is even}
questions.to_find: The language accepted by the given PDA by empty stack, and an informal description of its operation.

understand.plain: This PDA has two states: q_0 (push phase) and q_1 (pop/match phase). It works over alphabet {a,b} with stack alphabet {a,b,1} where 1 is the bottom-of-stack marker. The transitions in q_0 push every symbol onto the stack. At any point (nondeterministically), when the input symbol matches the stack top, the PDA can switch to q_1 and start popping. In q_1 each input symbol must match the stack top (popped). Finally, epsilon on 1 clears the bottom marker.

keywords.explain: A string where the first half equals the reverse of the second half, with even total length.
keywords.example: 'abba': first half 'ab', second half 'ba' = reverse of 'ab'.

keywords.explain: The PDA accepts when the input is fully consumed and the stack contains nothing (not even the bottom marker).
keywords.example: For 'abba': after processing, both 'a','b' on stack are popped and '1' is also cleared.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='130' cy='100' r='45' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q_0</text><text x='130' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>push</text><circle cx='400' cy='100' r='45' class='hi'/><text x='400' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q_1</text><text x='400' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>pop/match</text><defs><marker id='ar' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/></marker></defs><line x1='178' y1='100' x2='352' y2='100' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar)'/><text x='265' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>a,a->eps | b,b->eps</text><text x='265' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(midpoint guess)</text><path d='M110,55 A50,50 0 0,1 150,55' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar)'/><text x='130' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>push any</text><path d='M380,55 A50,50 0 0,1 420,55' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar)'/><text x='400' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>pop match</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-state PDA: q_0 pushes all input symbols. Nondeterministic midpoint transition from q_0 to q_1 pops a matching symbol. q_1 pops symbols matching each input, then clears the bottom marker '1'.

given.aim: Identify the language accepted by this two-state PDA (accepting by empty stack) and describe how it operates.

terms.meaning: delta(q_0, x, y) = {(q_0, xy)} for any x in {a,b} and y in {a,b,1}: the PDA pushes x on top of the current stack top y.
terms.example: Reading 'a' with '1' on stack: push 'a' -> stack becomes '1a' (1 at bottom, a on top).
terms.connects: These six transitions build up a copy of the input string on the stack.

terms.meaning: delta(q_0, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)} and delta(q_0, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}: when the current symbol matches the stack top, nondeterministically pop and switch to q_1.
terms.example: After pushing 'ab' for input 'abba', reading 'b' with 'b' on stack top triggers move to q_1.
terms.connects: This is the crucial nondeterministic guess of the midpoint of the even palindrome.

terms.meaning: delta(q_1, a, a) = {(q_1, epsilon)} and delta(q_1, b, b) = {(q_1, epsilon)}: match and pop each symbol. delta(q_1, epsilon, 1) = {(q_1, epsilon)}: clear bottom marker to achieve empty stack.
terms.example: In q_1, reading 'a' with 'a' on stack: pop, continue. At end, clear '1'.
terms.connects: After accepting by empty stack, the computation is complete.

given.plan: Trace the PDA on example strings. The push transitions in q_0 build the first half of the string on the stack. The midpoint transitions switch to q_1 at any point where the current character matches the stack top. The pop transitions in q_1 verify the second half is the mirror of the first half. Accept when stack is empty.

steps.title: Trace example: 'abba'
steps.apply: Start: (q_0, abba, 1). Read 'a': push -> (q_0, bba, 1a). Read 'b': push -> (q_0, ba, 1ab). Now read 'b': nondeterministically apply midpoint transition delta(q_0,b,b)=(q_1,eps) -> (q_1, a, 1a). Read 'a': delta(q_1,a,a)=(q_1,eps) -> (q_1, eps, 1). Apply delta(q_1,eps,1)=(q_1,eps) -> (q_1, eps, eps). Input exhausted, stack empty: ACCEPT.
steps.note: The string 'abba' is an even-length palindrome of length 4.

steps.title: Trace non-example: 'aba' (odd-length palindrome)
steps.apply: Start: (q_0, aba, 1). Push 'a': (q_0, ba, 1a). Nondeterministically try midpoint at 'b' with stack top 'a': 'b' != 'a', so midpoint transition doesn't fire. Push 'b': (q_0, a, 1ab). Try midpoint: 'a' with 'b' on top: no match. Push 'a': (q_0, eps, 1aba). Input exhausted but not in a state that can clear stack properly. All branches reject.
steps.note: Odd-length palindromes cannot be accepted, confirming the language is only even-length palindromes.

steps.title: Identify the language
steps.formula_raw: L = {w in {a,b}^+ | w = w^R and |w| is even}
steps.apply: The PDA pushes the first half of the input string onto the stack, then nondeterministically guesses the midpoint (only valid when the midpoint character matches the stack top character, i.e., the string has even-length symmetry), then pops while matching the second half. This precisely accepts all nonempty even-length palindromes over {a,b}.
steps.note: The bottom marker '1' ensures the stack never goes below the base; its removal at the end by delta(q_1,eps,1) completes the empty-stack acceptance.

solution.result: The language accepted is L = {w in {a,b}^+ | w = w^R and |w| is even}, i.e., the set of all nonempty even-length palindromes over {a,b}. The PDA works by: (b) pushing all input symbols in q_0 while nondeterministically guessing the midpoint by checking if the current input matches the stack top; once the midpoint is guessed, switching to q_1 to match and pop the remaining input against the stack, accepting by empty stack when both input and stack (including bottom marker) are exhausted.

questions.formula_note: NPDA construction for even palindromes: push first half nondeterministically, switch state at midpoint by popping a matching symbol, pop-match second half, clear bottom marker to accept by empty stack.

questions.question:
Let L_F be the set of all languages accepted by a PDA by final state and L_e be the set of all languages accepted by empty stack. Which of the following is true?
A. L_F = L_e
B. L_F subset L_e
C. L_e subset L_F
D. None
questions.to_find: The relationship between the class of languages accepted by PDA by final state (L_F) and by empty stack (L_e).

understand.plain: A pushdown automaton can be defined to accept strings in two different ways: either by reaching a designated final/accepting state, or by emptying the stack. The question asks whether these two acceptance modes yield the same class of languages. It is a fundamental theorem in formal language theory that both acceptance modes define exactly the same class of languages — the context-free languages.

keywords.explain: A PDA accepts a string if, after consuming all input, it is in one of the designated final (accepting) states (stack contents do not matter).
keywords.example: A PDA for {a^n b^n} with a final state q_accept reached after reading all b's.

keywords.explain: A PDA accepts a string if, after consuming all input, the stack is completely empty (state does not matter).
keywords.example: The same {a^n b^n} PDA modified to pop the bottom marker at the end instead of entering a final state.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='30' width='160' height='100' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L_F</text><text x='120' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Final State PDAs</text><rect x='260' y='30' width='160' height='100' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L_e</text><text x='340' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Empty Stack PDAs</text><text x='230' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>=</text><text x='230' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Same class: CFL</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two sets L_F and L_e shown as equal. Both represent the context-free languages — acceptance by final state and acceptance by empty stack are equivalent PDA acceptance modes.

given.aim: Determine the relationship between languages accepted by PDA by final state vs. empty stack.

terms.meaning: The class of all languages L such that there exists a PDA M where M accepts L by final state.
terms.example: Every CFL has a PDA that accepts it by final state.
terms.connects: By a standard construction, any PDA accepting by final state can be converted to one accepting by empty stack.

terms.meaning: The class of all languages L such that there exists a PDA M where M accepts L by empty stack.
terms.example: Every CFL has a PDA that accepts it by empty stack.
terms.connects: By a standard construction, any PDA accepting by empty stack can be converted to one accepting by final state.

terms.meaning: The class of languages generated by context-free grammars, equivalent to languages accepted by pushdown automata.
terms.connects: Both L_F and L_e equal the class of CFLs, hence L_F = L_e.

given.plan: Use the standard theorems: (1) Given a PDA M_F accepting by final state, construct a PDA M_e accepting the same language by empty stack. (2) Given a PDA M_e accepting by empty stack, construct a PDA M_F accepting the same language by final state. These two directions prove L_F = L_e.

steps.title: From empty stack to final state: L_e subset L_F
steps.apply: Given PDA M_e = (Q, Sigma, Gamma, delta, q_0, Z_0, phi) accepting by empty stack. Construct PDA M_F: add a new start state p_0, new final state p_f, new bottom marker X_0. From p_0, push X_0 and enter q_0 (epsilon-transition). When M_e would empty its stack (reaching X_0 on bottom), instead transition to p_f. This construction shows any language in L_e is also in L_F.
steps.note: The new bottom marker X_0 prevents the stack from truly becoming empty until the final state is reached.

steps.title: From final state to empty stack: L_F subset L_e
steps.apply: Given PDA M_F = (Q, Sigma, Gamma, delta, q_0, Z_0, F) accepting by final state. Construct PDA M_e: add new start state p_0, new state p_e, new bottom marker X_0. From p_0, push X_0 and enter q_0. From any final state q in F, add epsilon-transitions to p_e that pop everything off the stack (including X_0), accepting by empty stack.
steps.note: The popping transitions in p_e drain the stack symbol by symbol regardless of content.

steps.title: Conclude L_F = L_e
steps.apply: Since L_e subset L_F (step 1) and L_F subset L_e (step 2), we have L_F = L_e. Both equal exactly the class of context-free languages.
steps.note: This is a foundational theorem in formal language theory: the two PDA acceptance modes are equivalent.

questions.formula_note: Equivalence of PDA acceptance modes: acceptance by final state and acceptance by empty stack define the same class of languages (context-free languages). Standard conversion constructions prove L_F = L_e.
